THE TRIBUNAL’S ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS
173. The Tribunal notes that the Applicant made various claims in his Application as set out in
paragraph 106 above. In view of the Tribunal’s judgment in HS (Preliminary Objection) [2024],
the Tribunal will address the Applicant’s claims relating to (i) redundancy, (ii) breach of duty of
care, (iii) FY22 non-shortlisting, and (iv) EBC and data privacy.
REDUNDANCY DECISION
Scope of the Tribunal’s review in redundancy cases
174. The Tribunal recalls that “the decision to declare a staff member’s employment redundant
is an exercise of managerial discretion.” González Flavell, Decision No. 553 [2017], para. 137.
Accordingly, the Tribunal reviews such decisions for abuse of discretion, that is, the Tribunal
examines whether “a decision is arbitrary, discriminatory, improperly motivated or carried out in
violation of a fair and reasonable procedure.” Id., para. 137, citing Harou, Decision No. 273
[2002], para. 27.
175. The Tribunal has also recognized that, “[t]o substantiate a claim against a redundancy
decision, the initial burden of proof lies upon the applicant who must make a prima facie case of
abuse of power.” FI, Decision No. 625 [2020], para. 106, citing de Raet, Decision No. 85 [1989],
para. 57. At the same time, the Tribunal has recognized “that it may be ‘exceedingly difficult’ for
staff to substantiate an allegation of arbitrariness or lack of fairness amounting to an abuse of
discretion” (FI [2020], para. 106, quoting DD, Decision No. 526 [2015], para. 40), and considers

41
it “incumbent on the Tribunal to require the strictest observance of fair and transparent procedures
in implementing the Staff Rules dealing with redundancy” (FI [2020], para. 106, quoting Yoon
(No. 2), Decision No. 248 [2001], para. 28).
176. Finally, “[t]o be upheld, the redundancy decision in question must be based on a legitimate
rationale and must have been made in the interests of efficient administration.” FI [2020], para.
105.
Whether the redundancy decision had a reasonable and observable basis
177. As the Tribunal stated in EY, Decision No. 600 [2019], para. 104, “[t]he basic elements of
due process require that a staff member receives clear notification of the exact and correct Staff
Rule under which his or her employment is being terminated.” Pursuant to Staff Rule 7.01,
paragraph 9.02, in effect at the time of the redundancy decision:
Employment may become redundant when the Bank Group determines in the
interests of efficient administration, including the need to meet budgetary
constraints, that:
a) An entire organizational unit must be abolished;
b) A specific position or set of functions performed by an individual in an
organizational unit must be abolished;
c) The responsibilities of a position no longer match the skills and experience of
the incumbent and are unlikely to do so within a reasonable period of time; or
d) Types or levels of positions must be reduced in number.
178. The Applicant contends that the redundancy decision was the end result of a “cascading
series of due process violations and instances of unfair treatment.”
179. The Bank contends that the redundancy decision had a reasonable and observable basis.
180. The Tribunal will examine whether there was a legitimate business rationale for the
redundancy.

42
181. The record indicates that the Bank’s workforce planning exercise, which began in 2019,
sought to promote efficient administration by enhancing the Bank’s capacity to achieve favorable
outcomes for member countries and to strengthen its development impact and expertise on global
issues. The Tribunal notes that, as part of the July 2020 Global Practices realignment, technical
positions and their budgets within the Water GP were shifted from headquarters to regional offices,
placing responsibility for these roles with the regions. The Tribunal notes that this restructuring
led to major budget cuts and the elimination of several positions. As explained in the record, in the
Water GP specifically the process involved the competitive remapping of five operational and
technical staff to regional roles and the departure of eleven staff members across various grade
levels, including the Applicant.
182. The Tribunal observes that the Notice of Redundancy of 10 May 2022 states that the
redundancy decision was “taken in accordance with Staff Rule 7.01, paragraphs 9.02 (b. A specific
position or set of functions performed by an individual in an organizational unit must be abolished)
[…].” Further, the Tribunal observes the 26 July 2021 email communication from the Practice
Manager to the Applicant, stating, “I would like to once again confirm, that given the restructuring
of the Bank during the past two years, which shifted the Global Practice staff to the regions while
maintaining only a small team in the Global Unit, there is no longer a work program to support the
position to which you were assigned (on a temporary basis) as you transitioned from your posting
in ECA during your STD.” The email strongly encouraged the Applicant to place “priority
attention” in FY22 to finding a new assignment within the Bank or externally and further stated
that, if the Applicant was unable to find a position in the Water GP or other parts in the Bank by
the end of FY22, then Water GP management would discuss options with the Applicant for
separation from Bank employment.
183. The Tribunal also observes that the Bank’s business rationale for making the Applicant’s
position redundant is supported by the Proposed Staff Redundancy Form, which cites paragraph
9.02(b) as the relevant section of Staff Rule 7.01 for the redundancy decision and which states:
In July 2020, the Bank decided to realign the operational structure to the regions.
With this decision, the main operational and technical work on water resources
management in the Water Global Practice became the region’s responsibility. As a

43
result, five operational and technical positions in SWAGL moved from the global
unit to the regions through a competitive process, including 2 GH, 1 GG, and 2 GE
positions.
Following this restructure, the functions of a Senior WRM Specialist are no longer
part of the SWAGL business plan. Consequently, the unit no longer receives fund
allocation for this type of position. As a result, starting July 1, 2022, this position
will no longer exist in the Unit’s Workforce Plan and will be abolished under Staff
Rule 7.01, Ending Employment, para. 9.02(b) abolition of position.
184. The Tribunal finds this description is consistent with Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.02(b).
185. The Tribunal also notes the PRS Panel’s finding that the redundancy decision was based
on a reasonable and observable basis. In making this finding, the PRS Panel reviewed extensive
documentation and heard testimony from nine key witnesses involved in the matter. The PRS Panel
specifically noted the following testimonies: (i) the new Water Global Director, who testified that
the reason for the redundancy decision was the changed business needs of the Global Practices,
including the Water GP; (ii) the former Water Global Director, the Practice Manager, and the Senior
HR Business Partner, who testified that the reason for the redundancy decision was the realignment
of the Global Practices; and (iii) the Vice President, Sustainable Development, who testified that
because of the realignment there were severe budget cuts and a number of positions, including the
Applicant’s position, had to be abolished. The Tribunal observes that these testimonies corroborate
the business rationale contained in the Notice of Redundancy, Proposed Staff Redundancy Form,
and Practice Manager’s email correspondence of 26 July 2021.
186. Based on the above, the Tribunal concludes that the redundancy decision had a reasonable
and observable basis.
Whether proper procedure was followed in respect of the redundancy decision
187. Principle 2.1 of the Principles of Staff Employment provides that the Bank “shall at all
times act with fairness and impartiality and shall follow a proper process in [its] relations with staff
members.”

44
188. Pursuant to Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.03, in effect at the time of the redundancy decision:
A decision that a Staff Member’s employment is redundant will be made by a Vice
President, or where there is no Vice President, the responsible Department Director
responsible for administering the position, in consultation with the appropriate
Sector Board, Network, or other management group, where applicable, and with
the concurrence of the World Bank Group Human Resources Vice President or
his/her designee.
189. Further, pursuant to Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.04, in effect at the time of the redundancy
decision:
Staff Members will receive a written notice of redundancy. The notice will state
that the Staff Member’s employment is redundant, and that unless the Staff Member
is reassigned, the Staff Member’s employment will be terminated six months from
the effective date of the notice of redundancy.
190. The Applicant contends that the redundancy decision was made in violation of fair and
proper processes.
191. The Bank contends that, in addition to being based on legitimate business needs, the
redundancy decision followed all proper procedures in Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.
192. The Tribunal finds that the Bank’s position is plainly supported by the record. The Tribunal
observes that the redundancy decision was made by the appropriate decision-maker, the Vice
President, Sustainable Development, as stated in the Notice of Redundancy dated 10 May 2022,
and in accordance with Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.03.
193. Further, with respect to notice, the Notice of Redundancy carried an effective date of 1 July
2022 and was sent to the Applicant on 11 May 2022 by the Practice Manager. The Tribunal
observes that the Notice of Redundancy stated that the Applicant would be placed on
administrative leave for a six-month period from the effective date. The Tribunal further observes
that the Applicant went on a second STD, from 16 May 2022 to 16 May 2024, which delayed the
implementation of the redundancy decision for two additional years. On 16 May 2024, the
Applicant was placed on Administrative Leave “per the Notice of Redundancy issued in May

45
2022,” and his employment with the Bank ended six months later, on 16 November 2024, as
required by Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.04.
194. Moreover, as the Tribunal has previously noted, “[i]t is well established that, ‘[a]lthough
Staff Rule 7.01 does not provide for a specific advance warning about the issuance of a notice of
redundancy, a basic guarantee of due process requires that the staff member affected be adequately
informed with all possible anticipation of any problems concerning his career prospects, skills, or
other relevant aspect of his work.” FI [2020], para. 136, quoting Garcia-Mujica, Decision No. 192
[1998], para. 19.
195. In the present case, the Tribunal finds that this standard of due process, too, has been met.
As the Applicant himself acknowledges, he was first informed by the former Water Global
Director and the HR Manager on 26 May 2021 that his position within SWAGL was temporary
and that due to budgetary issues he would likely be separated from the Bank within twelve months
if he was unable to secure another position. The Tribunal notes that two months later, on 26 July
2021, the Applicant’s Practice Manager informed him that there was no longer a work program to
support the position to which he was assigned due to the Bank’s restructuring of the Global
Practices, and further stated, “If you are unable to find a position in the Water GP or in other parts
in the Bank by the end of this FY (June 30, 2022), we will discuss with you options for separation
from Bank employment.” Based on these facts, the Applicant received warning of possible
separation at least nine and a half months before receiving the Notice of Redundancy. Additionally,
considering the Applicant’s second two-year period on STD, approximately two and a half years
elapsed between receipt of the Notice of the Redundancy on 11 May 2022 and the end of the
Applicant’s employment at the Bank on 16 November 2024. Accordingly, the requirements of Staff
Rule 7.01, paragraphs 9.03 and 9.04, have been met.
196. Finally, the Tribunal notes the PRS Panel’s findings in Request for Review No. 573. In
considering all relevant information and documents related to the redundancy decision—including
the issuer of the decision, the Notice of Redundancy, and the “advanced notice of almost 10
months” the Applicant received before the Notice of Redundancy was issued—the PRS Panel

46
concluded that management followed a fair and proper process when making the redundancy
decision.
197. Based on the foregoing, the Tribunal finds that management followed proper procedure in
respect of the redundancy decision.
Whether the redundancy decision lacked transparency amounting to an abuse of discretion
198. The Applicant contends that management breached its duty of transparency by failing to
inform him in a timely manner that his reassignment to the SWAGL position on 1 November 2019
was temporary and slated for redundancy. The Applicant contends that management disclosed the
position’s temporary nature only in May 2021 and that he was not made aware until February 2022
that this status would also exclude him from participating in the batch rotation exercise process.
The Applicant further contends that his remapping breached the Memorandum of Extended Field
Assignment to Bishkek’s guarantee of reentry to a “suitable position” and Staff Rule 6.22,
paragraph 9.01, which he states “obligates the [Bank Group] to ‘seek[] to facilitate the [s]taff
[m]ember’s return to work prior to the completion of 24 months of STD, when […] the [s]taff
[m]ember is able to perform the material duties of [his/her] regular job.’”
199. The Bank rejects the Applicant’s contentions that designating his position as “temporary”
violated Staff Rules and that management used the position in SWAGL as a stopgap to redundancy.
The Bank states that the “temporariness” of the headquarters position in November 2019 meant
only that the Applicant would be reassigned to a field position, given that the Global Practices
realignment was in the planning stages in 2019 and rolled out via announcement in early January
2020. The Bank contends that it facilitated the Applicant’s return to work following his return from
STD leave in October 2020 by maintaining the Applicant’s title, salary, benefits, and workforce
integration, thereby fulfilling all obligations pursuant to the Applicant’s reentry guarantee and Staff
Rule 6.22, paragraph 9.01.
200. Further, the Bank contends that the Applicant’s claim of a breached reentry guarantee lacks
merit, as the Applicant omitted the key condition that reentry terms could change if his field

47
assignment was extended, which the Bank states occurred twice at the Applicant’s request. The
Bank contends that the Applicant’s requests to extend his field assignment nullified any original
reentry obligations, as Section 8 of the Memorandum of Extended Assignment to Bishkek allowed
the Bank to modify reentry terms upon such an extension, which it did.
201. The Tribunal recalls its position in HS (Preliminary Objection) [2024], para. 91, with
respect to the temporary assignment decision, stating:
[A]t the merits stage the Tribunal will not address the temporary assignment
decision as an independent and separate claim. The Tribunal, however, may
consider facts relating to the temporary assignment decision to the extent relevant
in its review of the Applicant’s redundancy claim at the merits stage.
202. Thus, the Tribunal’s inquiry on this contention will be limited to whether the Applicant’s
SWAGL position was slated for redundancy—or management otherwise designated the position
as a stopgap to redundancy—at the time of or before the Applicant was remapped to SWAGL.
203. The Tribunal observes that the Applicant was assigned to SWAGL on 1 November 2019,
while he was still on STD. The Tribunal further observes that, while there is no contemporaneous
evidence in the record demonstrating that the position was slated for redundancy at the time of the
Applicant’s 1 November 2019 remapping, there is subsequent documentation in the record that
sheds more light about the nature of the position and the time period it was likely slated for
redundancy. For example, the Tribunal observes that on 27 September 2023, in response to a
management inquiry from PRS, the Senior HR Business Partner wrote:
[The Applicant’s] position was not temporary in 2019. After the realignment in July
[2020], the business needs and the budget moved to the regions. Several units under
GGS had to reduce positions […] because of affordability and business needs. The
redundancy was about the position and not the person, and the decision was made
following the staff rule 7.01 in coordination with [HR].
204. The Tribunal observes that this communication appears to suggest that the position the
Applicant was holding in SWAGL became slated for redundancy sometime after 1 July 2020,
which was the effective date for the Global Practices realignment.

48
205. The Tribunal observes that discussions on workforce planning in the Sustainable
Development Vice Presidency continued in the Fall of 2020, which included discussions on staff
reductions to meet affordability targets. The Tribunal notes that, on 14 September 2020, the HR
Manager sent an email to the Director of Strategy and Operations, Sustainable Development Vice
Presidency, informing her that as part of the workforce planning exercise the Water GP would cut
three positions by the end of FY21. Two of those positions were cut in early September 2020 and
the third, a GH position, would be cut before the end of the fiscal year, resulting in 477 overall
positions in the Sustainable Development Vice Presidency. The Tribunal notes that later that day
the Director of Strategy and Operations replied, stating, “[W]e need to come down to 472. [The
Vice President, Sustainable Development] is not comfortable with this number, and it makes us
not affordable. So we need to have another round [of staff reductions].”
206. The Tribunal recalls that thereafter, on 15 October 2020, as with other staff members
affected by the realignment, the Applicant was offered a MAS or Early Out Option but rejected
the offer. The Tribunal notes that the MAS offer is the first instance in the record indicating that
the Applicant’s position could be slated for redundancy, which is approximately eleven and a half
months after his remapping to SWAGL. The Tribunal notes the Bank’s explanation that the
Applicant was offered the MAS because there were concerns that, as part of the realignment
exercise, the Applicant’s position would be declared redundant. This statement is corroborated by
the record, which demonstrates that as of 1 December 2020, nearly two months after the Applicant
returned from STD, he still did not have a full work program. The record shows that, on 11
November 2020, the Applicant wrote to the Practice Manager, stating, “I am in touch with [another
Practice Manager], and a work program is shaping up, albeit not yet full time.” On 1 December
2020, the Applicant wrote to the Practice Manager, stating, “We need to discuss this—[another
Practice Manager] can cover around 50% of my time for now, working towards some concrete
deliverables. There are openings for some cross–support on portfolios which I previously worked
on. [Is that an] [o]ption?”
207. The Tribunal observes that, a few months later on 26 May 2021, the Applicant states that
he was first informed that his SWAGL position was temporary and that he would likely be
separated from the Bank if he was unable to find a new position in the next twelve months. That

49
same message was delivered to the Applicant in writing by the Practice Manager on 26 July 2021,
and on 11 May 2022 the Applicant received the Notice of Redundancy. Notably, in the Proposed
Staff Redundancy Form, the reason for the redundancy decision does not appear to have been made
at or before the time the Applicant was remapped to SWAGL, but rather the decision appears to
have been made due to the Global Practices realignment, effective from 1 July 2020, and the
Applicant’s lack of work program since returning to work from STD in October 2020. The
Proposed Staff Redundancy Form states in relevant part:
Following [the Global Practices realignment in July 2020], the functions of a Senior
WRM Specialist are no longer part of the SWAGL business plan. Consequently,
the unit no longer receives fund allocation for this type of position. As a result,
starting July 1, 2022, this position will no longer exist in the Unit’s Workforce Plan
and will be abolished under Staff Rule 7.01, Ending Employment, para. 9.02(b)
abolition of position.
[The Applicant] was given cross-support opportunities to operations in the Africa
region, language courses (French), and some analytical work throughout this period
(since October 2020). However, these tasks are grossly insufficient and could not
justify an entire work program to cover the fixed costs of a full-time GG staff in
SWAGL.
208. Based on the evidence in the record, the Tribunal is satisfied that the Applicant’s SWAGL
position was not slated for redundancy on or before 1 November 2019 when he was mapped to the
position. Consequently, the Tribunal does not find that the redundancy decision lacked
transparency amounting to an abuse of discretion.
209. With respect to the Applicant’s contention that management disclosed his SWAGL
position’s temporary nature only in May 2021 and that he was not made aware until February 2022
that this status would also exclude him from participating in the batch rotation exercise process,
the Tribunal considers that this contention relates to the Applicant’s FY22 non-shortlisting claim
(as distinct from his redundancy claim), which has been addressed by PRS in Request for Review
No. 573, and will be addressed by the Tribunal in paragraphs 257–268 and 274–276 below. Further,
the Tribunal notes that the Applicant’s contention appears to be contradicted by his own statements
in a 15 October 2020 email to the Senior HR Business Partner, when he wrote:

50
HR and the global unit need some time to review the work program, since with the
regionalization, there are few positions with the global unit, and the majority of
positions are in the field mapped to the RVPs [Regional Vice Presidencies]. You
confirmed that I currently have a position at the unit of [the Practice Manager], but
[given] the push to put operational staff in the field, recommended to look for
options in the upcoming competitive batch-match in November–December. In the
meantime, you will reach out to [the Practice Manager] and [the former Water
Global Director] to look into the work program for me. You confirmed that
formally, with less than 3 years in the unit, I would not yet be eligible to apply, but
if I am interested in a field position, I could apply. I confirmed that I would be
interested to do so.
Whether the redundancy decision violated the Applicant’s reentry guarantee
210. Next, the Tribunal will examine the Applicant’s contention that the Bank failed to
meaningfully honor his reentry guarantee.
211. The Tribunal observes that the Applicant’s Memorandum of Extended Assignment to
Bishkek, dated 15 April 2015, stated that the term of the assignment was thirty-six months from
approximately 1 August 2015 and that, upon the Applicant’s satisfactory completion of the
assignment, the Water GP would provide a reentry for the Applicant to a “suitable position” in the
Water GP at his then current grade. Section 8 of the Memorandum of Extended Assignment to
Bishkek stated, “If this Extended Assignment is extended, the Bank Group reserves the right to
change re-entry conditions at the time of extension.” The Tribunal notes that, following the
Applicant’s start of STD in October 2018, he twice requested that the field assignment be extended,
from November 2018 to March 2019, and again in March 2019, the latter of which was approved
on an exceptional basis. Based on these extensions and the clear language under Section 8 of the
Memorandum of Extended Assignment to Bishkek, the Bank reserved the right at this point to
change reentry conditions.
212. The Tribunal notes that the Transition Memorandum of Temporary Assignment to Bishkek,
dated 12 November 2018, extending the field assignment from November 2018 to March 2019,
stated, “Your appointment will remain Open. […] Upon satisfactory completion of this assignment
your reentry guarantee will be the same as agreed in the beginning of your assignment.” The
Tribunal notes that the record contains no such transition memorandum for the field assignment

51
extension from March 2019 to November 2019 when the Applicant was remapped to SWAGL.
The Tribunal notes that, despite the extensions to the Applicant’s field assignment, he was still
reassigned to SWAGL, based in headquarters, on 1 November 2019. The Tribunal also notes that
management ensured that the Applicant retained his title, salary, and benefits, in line with the terms
of the reentry guarantee to provide reentry to a “suitable position” in the Water GP. Based on the
record, the Tribunal is satisfied that the Bank honored the Applicant’s reentry guarantee.
Whether the Applicant’s return to work was sufficiently facilitated by the Bank
213. Last, with respect to the Applicant’s return to work in October 2020 following STD leave,
the Tribunal is satisfied that the Bank complied with Staff Rule 6.22, paragraph 9.01, in effect at
the relevant time period, which states:
The Bank Group seeks to facilitate the staff member’s return to work prior to the
completion of 24 months of STD, when the Disability Administrator determines,
following receipt of a release to work form, that the staff member is able to perform
the material duties of his/her regular job.
214. The record is clear that Water GP management facilitated a return-to-work environment in
which the Applicant was able to perform the material duties of his regular job. The Tribunal
observes that management ensured that the Applicant retained his title, salary, and benefits, and
fully integrated into the workforce. Notwithstanding the Applicant’s difficulties in developing a
full work program, the record shows that Water GP management actively worked to tailor a work
program aligned with the Applicant’s career development, providing the Applicant with sufficient
support, granting opportunities for cross-support with African units in suitable tasks, while
simultaneously developing requisite skills essential for the Global Unit.
Whether the redundancy decision was based on retaliation and/or discrimination
215. The Tribunal has previously acknowledged that retaliation is prohibited under the Staff
Rules. In Bauman, Decision No. 532 [2016], para. 95, the Tribunal held:

52
As the Tribunal has frequently observed, the Staff Rules are clear that retaliation
against any person “who provides information regarding suspected misconduct or
who cooperates or provides information in connection with an investigation or
review of allegations of misconduct, review or fact finding, or who uses the Conflict
Resolution System” is prohibited. […] This prohibition extends also to retaliation
against any person who is believed to be about to report misconduct or believed to
have reported misconduct, even if such belief is mistaken.
216. Staff Rule 8.02 defines retaliation as “any direct or indirect detrimental action
recommended, threatened, or taken because an individual engaged in a [protected activity].” The
Bank’s Code of Ethics explains that a staff member is protected from retaliation if he or she
“reports an allegation of misconduct to INT [the Integrity Vice Presidency], EBC or a manager,
cooperates with or provides information in a related investigation, or uses any of the resources of
the [Bank’s] Internal Justice Services.”
217. The Tribunal has recognized that “it is not always easy for an applicant to produce evidence
to support a claim of retaliation.” Harou [2002], para. 68. Still, “[i]t is not enough for a staff
member to speculate or infer retaliation from unproven incidents of disagreement or bad feelings
with another person” (Bauman [2016], para. 99); an applicant must make a prima facie case “with
some evidence to show the […] retaliatory motives behind the impugned decision” (EY [2019]
para. 134). The Tribunal has explained that a prima facie case means, simply, that an applicant
“has pointed to facts that suggest that the Bank is in some relevant way at fault.” DP, Decision No.
547 [2016], para. 97, citing BI, Decision No. 439 [2010], para. 47. Once an applicant has
established a prima facie case, “the burden shifts to the Bank to disprove the facts or to explain its
conduct in some legally acceptable manner.” DJ (Merits), Decision No. 548 [2016], para. 58, citing
de Raet [1989], para. 57.
218. The Applicant contends that the entire chain of events from September 2019 onward
appears to have been calculated to usher him out of the Bank after he made clear that he believed
he had been mistreated with respect to the Romania mission and that he intended to continue
pressing this issue. Thus, to the Applicant, whatever legitimate interests the Bank may have had in
shifting resources to the regions, management’s use of this process as a means of pushing him out
of the Bank can only be “interpreted as pretextual” and, thus, improperly motivated.

53
219. The Bank contends that the Applicant has failed to establish a prima facie case of retaliation
in relation to the redundancy decision. Specifically, the Bank asserts that the Applicant’s claims
that the redundancy decision was motivated by retaliatory animus after he suggested that Mr. A
acted improperly in pressuring him to travel to Romania not only are speculative but also lack any
factual support in the record. The Bank maintains that the former Water Global Director and Mr.
A made considerable efforts to support the Applicant, including facilitating late applications,
coordinating medical accommodations, and providing career guidance, which contradict the
Applicant’s claims of pretext, ill motive, or a coordinated managerial effort to exclude him from
available positions within the Water GP, leading to his eventual departure.
220. The Tribunal will consider whether the Applicant has met his burden of proof with respect
to establishing a prima facie case that he was the subject of retaliation. The record shows that the
Applicant first reported concerns of discrimination, harassment, and hostile work environment to
EBC in April 2022 stemming from his treatment with respect to the Romania mission, his
reassignment to a temporary position, and Water GP management’s failure to include him in the
FY22 batch rotation exercise. This constituted a protected activity; however, the Tribunal notes
that there is no evidence in the record to show that Water GP management knew of the Applicant’s
report to EBC. The Tribunal notes that the Applicant was issued a Notice of Redundancy on 10
May 2022. In the instant case, the termination of the Applicant’s employment due to redundancy
could constitute an “adverse action” for purposes of a retaliation claim. The Tribunal notes, though,
that the Applicant must provide “some evidence to show the […] retaliatory motives behind the
impugned decision,” (EY [2019], para. 134) or otherwise “point[…] to facts that suggest that the
Bank is in some relevant way at fault” (DP [2016], para. 97).
221. The Tribunal finds that there is no evidence in the record showing retaliatory motives
behind the decision to abolish the Applicant’s position. As a preliminary matter, the Tribunal has
already established that there was a reasonable and observable basis for the redundancy decision
and also a legitimate business rationale, which was the realignment of the Global Practices at the
Bank and the lack of budget for the Applicant’s position. The Tribunal has also already established
that the Applicant’s remapping to SWAGL was a legitimate management decision based on several
factors, including the fact that the Applicant was returning from STD, the start of the realignment

54
exercise (which saw the focus shift from staff based in headquarters to field assignments), and that
headquarters was still under mandatory home-based work as a result of the global pandemic.
Despite the remapping, the Tribunal notes that the Applicant was supported with a work portfolio
consistent with his qualifications, provided with cross-support opportunities, encouraged to apply
for positions within the Bank, and provided with career counseling and French language courses
to make him more competitive.
222. Based on the foregoing, for purposes of the Applicant’s retaliation claim, there is no
evidence in the record that establishes that the Applicant has met his burden of proof with respect
to establishing a prima facie case.
223. The Tribunal will next consider the Applicant’s contention that he was discriminated
against because of his medical issues and his use of STD.
224. Principle 2.1 of the Principles of Staff Employment provides that the Bank “shall not
differentiate in an unjustifiable manner between individuals or groups within the staff.”
225. In Hitch, Decision No. 344 [2005], para. 43, the Tribunal explained that “discrimination
takes place where staff who are in basically similar situations are treated differently” (quoting
Crevier, Decision No. 205 [1999], para. 25), and in de Raet, [1989], para. 57, the Tribunal set out
the burden of proof with respect to allegations of discrimination:
[I]t is not the obligation of the Bank to demonstrate that there has been no
discrimination or abuse of power—not, that is, until an [a]pplicant has made out a
prima facie case or has pointed to facts that suggest that the Bank is in some relevant
way at fault. Then, of course, the burden shifts to the Bank to disprove the facts or
to explain its conduct in some legally acceptable manner.
See also DJ (Merits) [2016], para. 58. As the Tribunal further explained in AI, Decision No. 402
[2010], para. 42,
[t]here is no magic test; the proof needed to establish a prima facie case will vary
from case to case, depending on the facts and circumstances of each case. But as
indicated by the Tribunal in Bertrand, [Decision No. 81 [1989], para. 20,] the
[a]pplicant must at least provide “detailed allegations and factual support” for his

55
claim of racial discrimination. Applicants make prima facie cases of racial
discrimination if they adduce evidence from which the Tribunal can reasonably
infer such discrimination.
226. Further, as stated in Bodo, Decision No. 514 [2015], para. 77,
[t]he Tribunal recognizes that applicants may face challenges obtaining evidence
to support a discrimination claim. As was observed in Sekabaraga (No. 2)
[Decision No. 496 [2014]], para. 31 “[s]tatements indicating discrimination on the
part of the decision-maker and other forms of direct evidence are likely to be
available only in the most egregious cases. Claims must often rely principally on
circumstantial evidence from which discrimination may be inferred.” Yet, an
applicant asserting discrimination or retaliation must still make a prima facie case
with some evidence to show the discriminatory or retaliatory motives behind the
impugned decision.
227. With respect to his medical condition and disability status, the Applicant contends that,
prior to the Romania mission in September 2018, there was a clear, agreed upon understanding
that the Applicant would finish his Bishkek assignment, relocate to Washington, D.C. while
remaining mapped to ECA, and spend necessary time building relationships and reintegrating with
the Water GP before moving on to another region. However, according to the Applicant, it was
only after it became clear that his medical condition and resulting disability leave would continue
well into 2019 that the situation abruptly changed. The Applicant asserts that this was when, “out
of the blue,” management informed him—without any contemporaneous explanation of why—
that he was being remapped to SWAGL. To the Applicant, the only plausible explanation is that
Mr. A wanted the Applicant out of his unit once it became clear that his medical issues and
disability leave would be ongoing, and the former Water Global Director saw the mandate to shift
resources out of the Water GP and into the regions as an opportunity to remove the Applicant from
ECA—because of his disability status—without rotating him into another region.
228. The Bank rejects the Applicant’s suggestion of the existence of an elaborate conspiracy
against him “allegedly involving a Senior Managing Director, a Vice President, multiple Global
Directors, several managers, at least three HRBPs, and numerous other Bank colleagues” to
remove him from the institution based on his extended STDs and sick leave. The Bank contends
that the Applicant has failed to produce any evidence in support of this claim.

56
229. In the instant case, the Tribunal finds that there is insufficient evidence in the record to
indicate that the Applicant was treated differently from others in the same position. The record
indicates that the Global Practices realignment—designed to address organizational priorities and
enhance the alignment of resources with operational needs in the regions—resulted in significant
budget reductions and the elimination of several positions. As previously stated, within the Water
GP, the process involved the competitive remapping of five operational and technical staff to
regional roles and the departure of eleven staff members across various grade levels. Of the eleven
exited staff members, two were at Grade Level GG, including the Applicant. Further, as with other
staff members affected by the reorganization, the Applicant was offered a MAS or Early Out option
but declined it. The Tribunal considers that, in these circumstances, these facts do not support the
Applicant’s contention that he was singled out, nor do they suggest that the Applicant was treated
differently from other similarly situated staff members.
230. Further, the Tribunal notes the PRS testimonies of the former Water Global Director; the
Vice President, Sustainable Development; and the Practice Manager that the Applicant’s health
issues were not discussed by management and did not play any role in the redundancy decision, as
well as the PRS Panel’s finding that the Applicant’s health issues did not play any role in the
redundancy decision.
231. Based on the foregoing, the Tribunal finds that the Applicant has not provided any evidence
of discrimination specific to his case and, as such, has failed to make a prima facie case of
discrimination.
Whether the Bank provided the Applicant with support in finding alternative employment
232. Principle 7 of the Principles of Staff Employment, in effect at the relevant time period,
stated in pertinent part that staff members may be separated from the Bank
when the Organizations determine that a position or positions are no longer
necessary, or that the responsibilities of a position have changed so that the staff
member is not qualified to fill it, provided that no vacant position in the same type
of appointment exists for which the Organizations determine that the staff member

57
is eligible and has the required qualifications or for which he or she can be retrained
in a reasonable period of time.
233. Further, pursuant to Staff Rule 7.01, paragraph 9.06, in effect at the time of the redundancy
decision:
Following the effective date of the notice of redundancy, the Bank Group will assist
redundant Staff in seeking another position within the Bank Group by providing
access to career counseling services. Staff are responsible for applying to existing
vacancies in the World Bank Group’s recruitment portal. Placement also may be
offered in a vacant lower level job in accordance with Staff Rule 5.06. “Assignment
to Lower Level Positions.”
234. In Arellano (No. 2), Decision No. 161 [1997], para. 42, the Tribunal found that the Bank’s
obligation in this respect “is not to reassign staff members whose employment was declared
redundant under Staff Rule 7.01 but to try genuinely to find such staff members alternative
positions for which they are qualified.” Additionally, in Marshall, Decision No. 226 [2000], para.
45, the Tribunal noted that “the job-search exercise requires efforts from both sides.”
235. The Applicant contends that management breached its obligation to make a genuine effort
to find the Applicant an alternative position for which he was qualified that would allow him to
avoid redundancy.
236. The Bank contends that it provided the required assistance for alternative employment to
the Applicant.
237. The record shows that, following the Practice Manager’s 26 July 2021 email warning the
Applicant of possible separation, management offered and provided the Applicant with
opportunities to improve his skills to become more competitive in his job search. The Tribunal
notes the PRS testimony of the former Water Global Director who testified that she encouraged
the Applicant to avail himself of the Bank’s career counseling services to improve his interview
skills. Furthermore, the record shows that the Bank provided the Applicant with retraining
opportunities, including French language classes, which were fully funded by the Bank. In addition
to these efforts, the Tribunal notes that the Applicant was provided with cross-support

58
opportunities to collaborate on operations in the Africa Region, providing him with opportunities
to maintain visibility within the Bank, collaborate with other teams, learn new skills, and network.
238. The record does not support the Applicant’s contention that he did not receive any support
from management in finding other opportunities within the Bank. The Tribunal notes that the PRS
record contained an excerpt from the Talent Board’s Meeting Minutes of 31 August 2021 stating
that Water Practice Managers were encouraged to give assignments to the Applicant. The Tribunal
observes that on 17 December 2021, in response to the Applicant’s request for advice on the FY22
batch rotation exercise process, the former Water Global Director advised the Applicant to contact
hiring managers directly for information on job listings because they were “in charge of the
process” and to seek feedback from the chair of the panel for the positions for which he had been
interviewed. The Tribunal observes that, on the same date, the Practice Manager urged the
Applicant to submit his applications for the FY22 batch rotation exercise because that day was the
application deadline. The Tribunal notes further testimony to the PRS Panel from the Practice
Manager, who testified that she had been encouraging the Applicant to apply for positions since
2021, and the HR Manager, who testified that the Practice Manager had asked him for support for
the Applicant to help him with finding another assignment at the Bank. Based on the foregoing,
the Tribunal is satisfied that the Bank has fulfilled its obligations in respect of the Staff Rules in
providing the Applicant with support in finding alternative employment.
DUTY OF CARE
239. The Applicant contends that the Bank breached its duty of care to provide a safe and healthy
work environment, specifically through Mr. A’s actions, including pressuring him to travel to
Romania shortly after surgery, failing to assess or provide reasonable accommodations, and
continuing to push for his participation despite clear medical limitations.
240. The Bank contends that the Applicant has presented no credible evidence that he was
pressured by Mr. A to attend the mission in Romania in September 2018. The Bank contends that
the only evidence the Applicant presents to support his contention is primarily a phone call with
Mr. A on 4 September 2018, during which the Applicant contends that Mr. A pressured him to

59
travel, and one sentence in the Applicant’s draft FY18 OPE. With respect to the draft FY18 OPE
comments, the Bank contends that those comments were shared with the Applicant during the
Romania mission on 18 September 2018, and therefore it is “impossible” that the comments
influenced the Applicant’s decision to undertake the mission because he was already in Romania
by the time he could have read it. The Bank contends that, if Mr. A’s assignment of the Romania
mission involved no coercion, then the Applicant’s related claims must fail, as there is no evidence
of misconduct to support retaliation, harassment, or hostile work environment allegations.
241. The Tribunal finds no evidence in the record to suggest that the Applicant was unduly
pressured by Mr. A to travel to Romania. The Tribunal observes that on 9 August 2018, prior to the
Applicant’s Achilles surgery, the Applicant and Mr. A exchanged correspondence about the
upcoming Romania mission. The Applicant stated that he could probably attend the mission on
crutches, but he would update Mr. A after speaking with his doctor. Mr. A replied, stating, “Thanks
and that would be perfect—your recovery getting priority of course so let’s not force it. Will await
your update then.” The Tribunal notes that on 15 August 2018, after speaking to his doctor, the
Applicant informed Mr. A that he could make the Romania mission. He had surgery five days
later.
242. The Tribunal observes that, on 4 September 2018, the Applicant wrote to Mr. A, updating
him on his recovery from surgery and stating, “I don’t think I can pull off Romania in mid
September, since I have to keep my leg up. I was optimistic initially, but having done this now for
10 days, I noticed how every small thing takes a big effort all of a sudden. We have to discuss how
to do this.” Thereafter, the Applicant states that Mr. A called him and reiterated that the Romania
mission was a business priority, and that the Applicant’s presence was required. The Applicant
states that this conversation, coupled with recent OPE feedback, made him feel as if he had no
choice but to attend the Romania mission despite the reservations he voiced to Mr. A via email.
243. The Tribunal observes that, on 9 September 2018, the Applicant emailed HSD asking for
advice regarding his upcoming travel. The Senior Occupational Health Specialist wrote back,
stating:

60
My advice is that you should avail yourself of sick leave and short term disability,
until such time you are able to mobilize with minimal assistance and you[r]
physicians are in agreement with the amount of travel and activity that you have
proposed is in accordance with your post-operative recovery.
244. The Tribunal notes that the Applicant responded the same day, stating, “In case the doctor
recommends not to go on [the] business trip to Romania, and I decide to go anyway in order to
accommodate the request of management, would this affect my insurance coverage in case of
medical complications while on business trip?” The Tribunal notes that the Senior Occupational
Health Specialist replied, “I would take as much time as is medically necessary so as not to
compromise your recent surgery and recovery.” Thereafter, the Tribunal notes that the Applicant
continued proactively making plans and organizing medical accommodations for the Romania
mission. Based on these facts, the Tribunal is not convinced that the Applicant was unduly
pressured into traveling to Romania. The Tribunal also finds that the Applicant has presented no
prima facie evidence that Mr. A harassed or created a hostile work environment for the Applicant
with respect to the Romania mission.
245. Further, the Tribunal notes that in June 2018, when the Applicant’s injury was not as severe
as it was post-surgery in September 2018, the Applicant on his own canceled a mission to Baku
on account of his injury. The Tribunal is not persuaded that, at the time the Applicant made the
decision to proceed with the mission to Romania, he was precluded from weighing the risks and
canceling the mission as he had done with Baku. Based on the record, the Tribunal finds that the
Bank did not breach its duty of care to the Applicant with respect to the Romania mission.
FY22 NON-SHORTLISTING DECISION
246. The Tribunal has recognized that selection decisions are discretionary decisions of the
Bank. The Tribunal has consistently held that it will not overturn a discretionary managerial
decision, unless it is demonstrated that the exercise of discretion was “arbitrary, discriminatory,
improperly motivated, carried out in violation of a fair and reasonable procedure, or lack[ed] a
reasonable and observable basis, constitute[d] an abuse of discretion, and therefore a violation of

61
a staff member’s contract of employment or terms of appointment.” AK, Decision No. 408 [2009],
para. 41; see also Desthuis-Francis, Decision No. 315 [2004], para. 19.
247. In Riddell, Decision No. 255 [2001], para. 23, the Tribunal stated:
[N]o staff member has a right to be selected to a particular position or to be included
in a list of candidates for a position. The decision to select an applicant for a
particular position, or to include him or her in a list of candidates, is discretionary
and the Tribunal will not overturn such a decision unless it finds that it is tainted by
bias or abuse of discretion.
248. Furthermore, in Jassal, Decision No. 100 [1991], para. 37, the Tribunal held:
It is not for the Tribunal, in assessing the validity of the selection or non-selection
of a staff member, to undertake its own examination of that staff member’s record,
or a criterion-by-criterion assessment of his or her qualifications. That is for the
Bank to do in the first instance, subject to review by the Tribunal only for abuse of
discretion. But the Tribunal is charged with determining whether the Bank’s
decision was the product of bias, prejudice, arbitrariness, manifest
unreasonableness, or unfair or improper procedure. Thus, if the Bank’s conclusion
regarding the [a]pplicant’s qualifications for selection […] altogether lacks support
in factual evidence or reasonable inference, that conclusion must be found to be an
abuse of discretion.
249. The Applicant contends that the Bank abused its discretion and violated his contract of
employment or terms of appointment in three ways: (i) it failed to provide a reasonable and
observable basis for the non-shortlisting decision; (ii) it failed to follow a fair and proper process
in making the non-shortlisting decision; and (iii) it made the non-shortlisting decision in bad faith.
The Tribunal will examine these three main claims of the Applicant.
Whether there was a reasonable and observable basis for the non-shortlisting decision
250. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel reviewed the record, including the TORs for the
three positions the Applicant applied to and the corresponding shortlisting report, to determine the
reasons for the non-shortlisting decision.

62
251. The Tribunal observes that the shortlisting report stated the following reasons for non-
shortlisting the Applicant for the Antananarivo position: “Does not have strong track record on
utility management, unlike other candi[d]ates. Already interviewed candidate several times during
this year for other positions.” The Tribunal further observes that the shortlisting report stated the
following reasons for non-shortlisting the Applicant for the Bamako position: “[M]eets basic
criteria although very [weak] on FCV and no real AFR experience. Basic French and fluency is
needed in Africa West.”
252. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel concluded that management exercised its
discretion appropriately and that it was reasonable and observable for management to not shortlist
the Applicant for the Antananarivo and Bamako positions due to the lack of strong technical skills,
strong FCV experience, and fluency in French.
253. The Tribunal observes that, with respect to the Senior Water Resource Management
Specialist position based in Yaoundé, the TOR listed the following specific requirements:
Specialized skills: Deep integrative and operational skills, spanning Water in
agriculture, watershed management, large complex water development
infrastructure. Experience of collaborating with Ag, ENB [Environment, Natural
Resources and Blue Economy Global Practice] and URL [Urban, Disaster Risk
Management, Resilience, and Land Global Practice]. A track record in effectively
developing and leading complex client engagements, operations and ASA
[Advisory Services and Analytics] and working across GPs. Demonstrated
experience in translating analytical work into policy recommendations and high-
level policy engagement, including through DPOs [Development Policy
Operations] and contribution to other GP’s operations, highly desirable.
254. The Tribunal observes that the shortlisting report stated the following reasons for non-
shortlisting the Applicant for the Yaoundé position: “Meets basic criteria although very weak on
FCV and no real AFR experience. Basic French and fluency is needed in Africa West.”
255. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel, in its assessment of the Yaoundé position, found
that there was no match between the selection criteria in the TOR and the reasons for non-
shortlisting the Applicant provided in the shortlisting report. Given that the Applicant was
evaluated against selection criteria which were not included in the TOR, i.e., French language

63
skills and International Development Association (IDA)-FCV experience, the PRS Panel
concluded that the non-shortlisting decision regarding the Yaoundé position lacked an observable
basis.
256. Based on the submissions of the parties and the record as a whole, the Tribunal agrees with
the above findings of the PRS Panel and concludes that the non-shortlisting decision regarding the
Yaoundé position lacked a reasonable and observable basis.
Whether the Bank followed a fair and proper process in making the FY22 non-shortlisting
decision
257. The Tribunal observes that on 7 December 2021 the Water GP launched its annual batch
rotation exercise for FY22. The communication to staff, which was received by the Applicant,
included links to the new mobility framework and the water competencies framework. The email
stated in part that the cluster recruitment was open to all staff professionally mapped to Water, the
hiring managers would draw up shortlists of the top qualified applicants for each position, and the
Talent Board would ensure that each staff needing to rotate was included in at least one shortlist.
258. The Tribunal observes that, on 14 December 2021, the Applicant emailed the former Water
Global Director, copying the Practice Manager, requesting a meeting to receive feedback and
guidance on the batch rotation exercise process. The Applicant also stated his intention to apply
for three positions in Bamako, “Beijing/HQ,” and Yaoundé. Three days later, on 17 December
2021, the former Water Global Director responded to the Applicant, advising him to contact the
hiring managers directly in case he needed information on the job listings. The former Water
Global Director also suggested seeking feedback from the chair of the panel for the positions for
which the Applicant interviewed.
259. The Tribunal notes that, on 17 December 2021, the Applicant emailed the Practice Manager
to confirm his intention to apply for positions in Yaoundé, Bamako, and “HQ,” and requested any
guidance the Practice Manager might have regarding his applications. The Tribunal further notes
that, later that same day, the Practice Manager replied, stating, “Thanks for letting me know on

64
your choices. Please go ahead in submitting your applications. Today is the last date.” Thereafter,
the Applicant submitted applications for positions based in Antananarivo, Bamako, and Yaoundé.
260. The Tribunal notes that on 28 January 2022 the Applicant received an automated email
from Compass stating that he was not shortlisted for any of the three positions he had applied for.
261. The Tribunal observes that, according to the Applicant, he met with the former Water
Global Director, the HR Manager, and the Practice Manager on 16 February 2022, and that the
former Water Global Director informed him that the batch rotation exercise rules did not apply to
him because of the temporary nature of his SWAGL position and that this is why he was not
shortlisted for at least one position.
262. The Tribunal observes that an undated shortlisting report, provided by the former Water
Global Director to PRS, stated various reasons for the non-shortlisting of the Applicant. For the
Yaoundé position, the report states, “Meets basic criteria although very weak on FCV and no real
AFR experience. Basic French and fluency is needed in Africa West.”
263. The Tribunal observes that, during the PRS hearing, the PRS Panel noted that the
requirements for IDA-FCV experience and fluency in French were not listed in the TOR for the
Yaoundé position. The PRS Panel stated that, while it recognized management’s discretion in
determining the needs and requirements of the position it planned to fill, the TOR for a position
should list the minimum requirements for the position, and that management should evaluate the
candidates against the selection criteria listed in the TOR. The PRS Panel found that the TOR for
the Yaoundé position should have clearly stated the minimum requirements for the position,
including the requirements for fluency in French and IDA-FCV experience. The PRS Panel found
that the Applicant was evaluated against selection criteria which were not included in the TOR,
and it therefore concluded that management did not meet the necessary standard of transparency
in the shortlisting process for the Yaoundé position.
264. Additionally, the Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel noted that the Applicant stated that
he was supposed to rotate or face redundancy and stated, based on the former Water Global

65
Director’s 7 December 2021 email, that he should have been included in at least one shortlist
during the FY22 batch rotation exercise. The former Water Global Director and new Water Global
Director testified that the Applicant was not eligible for the batch rotation exercise because he did
not hold a permanent position, and thus the mobility rules, including the one-shortlist guarantee,
did not apply. The former Water Global Director explained that the Applicant’s position became
temporary due to the realignment of the Global Practices, which started in FY20, and that HR
advised management that the Applicant needed to have a position upon his return from STD, and
therefore his position became a “placeholder.”
265. The PRS Panel also noted that the Applicant questioned why he received the 7 December
2021 email and was encouraged to apply if he was not part of the batch rotation exercise. The
former Water Global Director explained to the PRS Panel that the email went to all Water GP staff
except consultants and that staff who were in a position for a certain number of years or seeking
promotion were eligible to apply for positions in the batch.
266. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel noted that the former Water Global Director’s
email of 7 December 2021 did not make any distinction between staff on permanent or temporary
positions as a condition to participate in the batch rotation exercise, or for inclusion in at least one
shortlist. The PRS Panel also noted that the Applicant was informed that the one-shortlist rule did
not apply to him on 16 February 2022, which was after the email of 7 December 2021, and after
management had encouraged him to apply for the positions in the batch rotation exercise. The PRS
Panel was of the view that, by sending the aforementioned email to the Applicant and encouraging
him to apply for the positions in the batch rotation exercise, management made him believe that
he was eligible to participate in the FY22 batch rotation exercise and that he would be shortlisted
for at least one position. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel noted that management was
obliged to ensure that, in accordance with the duty of care owed to staff members, the shortlisting
process was implemented in a manner which was fair to all candidates, including the Applicant.
The PRS Panel further noted that the Applicant, as a staff member in good standing, had every
right to be part of the batch rotation exercise. Finally, the PRS Panel concluded that management
did not act in a fair and transparent manner because it denied the Applicant the opportunity to be
included in one shortlist for a position.

66
267. The Tribunal observes that, in sum,
the [PRS] Panel found that, by not including the requirements of IDA-FCV
experience and fluency in French in the TOR for the Yaoundé position, and yet not
shortlisting [the Applicant] on these grounds, management did not follow a due and
fair process. Additionally, the [PRS] Panel found that, by advising [the Applicant]
of his non-eligibility to participate in the FY22 Water GP batch recruitment only
after he was not shortlisted, the process lacked transparency and fairness.
268. Based on the submissions of the parties and the record as a whole, the Tribunal agrees with
the above findings of the PRS Panel and concludes that the Bank failed to follow a fair and proper
process in the selection process.
Whether the FY22 non-shortlisting decision was made in bad faith
269. The Tribunal notes the PRS Panel’s finding that the Applicant did not provide any evidence
in support of his claim that the non-shortlisting decision was made in bad faith.
270. Further, the Tribunal notes that the PRS Panel questioned each witness to assess potential
bias or improper motive in the Applicant’s non-shortlisting. The former Water Global Director
stated that she was not involved in shortlisting and attended only the Talent Board discussion, a
claim supported by the Senior HR Business Partner, a Practice Manager in the Water GP covering
the East Africa Region, and a Practice Manager in SWAGL. The Practice Manager in the Water
GP covering the East Africa Region also confirmed that the former Water Global Director made
no remarks about the Applicant during the Talent Board meeting. All four witnesses testified that
the Applicant was treated like other candidates and was not shortlisted based on a comparative
assessment of his skills and experience.
271. The Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel concluded that that there was no evidence of
bias, bad faith, or an improper motive in the non-shortlisting decision.
272. The Tribunal notes that the Applicant raised concerns about the FY22 non-shortlisting
decision with EBC in April 2022 and that, on 7 December 2022, EBC closed the case after a

67
preliminary review, advising the Applicant that PRS was the appropriate forum for employment-
related issues such as redundancy and non-selection.
273. Based on the submissions of the parties and the record as a whole, the Tribunal agrees with
the above findings of the PRS Panel and concludes that the FY22 non-shortlisting decision was
not made in bad faith.
The question of additional compensation
274. The Tribunal will now consider whether the Applicant is entitled to additional
compensation above the four months’ net salary awarded following the PRS process.
275. The Tribunal notes that the PRS Panel found that the non-shortlisting for the Yaoundé
position lacked transparency and an observable basis, though it was not made in bad faith, and
recommended one month’s net salary as compensation. The Tribunal also notes that the PRS Panel
concluded that management failed to act fairly by not including the Applicant in at least one FY22
batch rotation exercise shortlist and recommended an additional three months’ net salary in
compensation. The Tribunal notes that the Senior HR Business Partner, who was present at the
Talent Board meetings, testified to PRS that the reasons for non-shortlisting the Applicant for any
of the three positions were that he did not have strong technical skills and FCV experience, and
that other candidates had stronger qualifications. The HR Manager testified to PRS that the
Applicant’s non-shortlisting came about because he did not meet the technical requirements for
the three positions, not because he was on a temporary position. The Tribunal also considers that,
based on the undated shortlisting report for the Antananarivo, Bamako, and Yaoundé positions in
the FY22 batch rotation exercise, the shortlisted candidates were considerably stronger candidates
than the Applicant. Given this context, even if the Applicant was shortlisted for one of the three
positions, the likelihood of success for the Applicant to secure such a position would have been
minimal.
276. Based on these considerations, the Tribunal is satisfied that the four months of
compensation the Applicant received following the PRS process is fair and adequate.

68
EBC AND DATA PRIVACY
277. The Applicant contends that EBC abused its discretion by not investigating the Applicant’s
allegations of discrimination, retaliation, harassment, and hostile work environment.
278. The Bank contends that the Applicant has failed to make a prima facie case of any
wrongdoing by Mr. A in connection with the Romania mission, nor has the Applicant demonstrated
any retaliatory motive or improper managerial conduct.
279. For additional context into the Applicant’s misconduct allegations, the Tribunal will first
look at the PRS Panel’s review of the Applicant’s retaliation and discrimination allegations. The
Tribunal observes that the PRS Panel noted that
[…] [the Applicant] did not provide any supporting evidence that the Redundancy
Decision was retaliatory, discriminatory, or made in bad faith. Without any
elaboration on his claims or evidence of actual or perceived retaliation or
discrimination by management, [the Applicant] has given the [PRS] Panel little to
deliberate on. The [PRS] Panel found that [the Applicant] did not establish “a prima
facie” case of discrimination or retaliation.
280. Next, the Tribunal will look into the Applicant’s misconduct claims with EBC. The
Tribunal notes that the Applicant reported various misconduct claims, including discrimination, to
EBC in April and June 2022. The Tribunal observes that on 7 December 2022 EBC wrote to the
Applicant informing him that it was closing its case following a preliminary review of his
misconduct allegations. EBC stated that with respect to his misconduct allegations, which included
discrimination and unauthorized disclosure of confidential information, there was insufficient
evidence of a violation of staff rules to warrant further review. The Tribunal notes that, on 23
February 2024, the Applicant wrote to EBC requesting that it reopen its review into his claims
based on additional factual developments since December 2022. On 17 April 2024, EBC denied
the Applicant’s request, stating that it “[did] not see any basis to re-open the review.” EBC further
stated, “The concerns described […] are substantively similar to those we reviewed in 2022 and
further to our review of the information you have shared, we have determined that our previous
conclusions remain valid.”

69
281. The Tribunal has analyzed the Applicant’s retaliation and discrimination allegations above
at paragraphs 215–231 and determined that the Applicant has failed to make a prima facie case of
retaliation and discrimination with respect to the impugned redundancy decision. Similarly, the
Tribunal has analyzed the Applicant’s harassment and creation of hostile work environment
allegations above at paras. 239–245 and determined that the Applicant has failed to make a prima
facie showing.
282. Based on the record, including the conclusions reached by the PRS Panel and EBC, the
Tribunal finds that there is insufficient evidence to support a finding that the redundancy decision
was improperly motivated by retaliation or discrimination. The Tribunal also finds that there is
insufficient evidence to support a finding that Mr. A harassed or created a hostile work environment
for the Applicant in the context of the Romania mission in September 2018. The Tribunal also
concludes that EBC has not abused its discretion in this case.
283. With regard to the Applicant’s contention that the Tribunal has jurisdiction to review his
data privacy claim, the Tribunal looks to the Personal Data Privacy Request and Review
Mechanisms Procedures, in effect at the relevant time, which state at Section III, C(3)(a):
Following the First Tier Reviewer’s determination, the Data Subject may submit
an appeal in connection with the Call for Review to:
The World Bank Administrative Tribunal in the case of Data Subjects having
standing before the World Bank Administrative Tribunal pursuant to the
requirements of and for decision in relation to the Call for Review in accordance
with the Statute of the World Bank Administrative Tribunal.
284. The Tribunal finds that the language in this section plainly provides the Tribunal with
jurisdiction to hear the Applicant’s appeal. The Tribunal notes that the First Tier Reviewer, who
interviewed five people, found no evidence that the Applicant’s personal data was improperly
processed or shared, including information about his mental health. The Tribunal observes that the
investigation showed that any sharing of personal data was done by the Applicant himself, and that
there was no violation of the Privacy Policy’s standards for legitimate, fair, and transparent
processing.

70
285. Based on the record, including the conclusions reached by EBC and the First Tier
Reviewer, the Tribunal agrees with the First Tier Reviewer’s conclusion that there was no violation
of the Bank’s Data Privacy Policy with respect to the Applicant.
DECISION
The Application is dismissed.
